title: 第 35 節
en: 6 Brahmabindu Up. 12. 6 Cf. Bhag. Gita V, T& S 258 INDIAN PHILOSOPHY.

in worms and insects, in the outcast, in the dog and the elephant, in beasts, cows, gadflies, and gnats. and pearls, jewels, corals, porcelain, and silver, thus is one and the same Self to be known as dwelling everywhere in cows, men, and in elephants, deer/ £c.
We see in these extracts a mixture of Ved&nta and S&mkhya terms and ideas ; and in verse 10 the two views of Brahman being one, and the Purusha being many, are given in the same breath.
Early Relation "between Vedanta and Sambhya.

The relation between Samkhya and Vedanta during the Upanishad-period is by no means clear. Most scholars seem to regard it as a kind of syncretism, but it may also represent to us a period of philosophic thought when these two views of the world were not yet finally differentiated, and were not felt to be altogether incompatible. Though there is in the TJpanishads which we possess a decided preponderance of a Vedantic interpretation of the world, the S&mkhya philosophers are not altogether wrong when they maintain that their view also can be supported by Vedic authority. All these views were at first no more than guesses at truth, gropings in the dark ; but the idea that if the one was right the other must be wrong, belongs decidedly to a later period, to that of systematised and controversial philosophy. There are certain technical terms, such as Purusha, Buddhi, Gu?ias, &c., which are looked upon as the peculiar property of the Samkhya, and others, such as Atman, Brahman, Avidy&, May&, &c., which remind us at once of the Ved&nta-philosophy ; but even these terms are used far more freely in the Br^hmams and Upanishads than in the Darsanas, nor are they always used in the same sense or in the same order by earlier and later authorities.
Thus we read in the K&£Aaka Up. Ill, 10, n : —

< Beyond the senses are the objects (Artha), beyond the objects is the mind (Manas), beyond Athe mind is intellect (Buddhi), the Great Self (MaMn Atma) is beyond the intellect. Beyond the Great there is the Undeveloped DELATION BETWEEN VBDANTA AND SAJfKHYA. 259 (Avyakta), beyond the undeveloped there is the Purusha. Beyond the Purusha there is nothing, that is the goal, the highest point.'
In the same Upanishad, VI, 7, 8, we read : —
highest being (Sattvam TJttamam), higher than that being is the great Self (Mahan Atma), beyond this great (Self) is the highest, the Undeveloped.

Beyond the Undeveloped is the Purusha, the all-pervading and imperceptible. Every creature that knows him is liberated, and obtains immortality/ The successive development, as here described, is not in strict accordance with the systematic Samkhya, but still less does it represent to us Vedantic ideas. Even the two accounts, as given in the same Upanishad, vary slightly, showing to us how little of technical accuracy there was as yet during the Upanishad-period. We get — III, 10, ii. VI, 7, 8.
1. Indriyas. Indriyas.
2. Arthas.
3. Manas. Manas.
4. Buddhi.A Sattvani Uttamam.
5. Mahan Atma. Mah&n Atma.
6. Avyakta. Avyakta.
7. Purusha. Purusha.

The omission of the Arthas as objects would not signify, because, as Indriyarthas, they are implied by the Indriyas or senses. But why should Buddhi, generally the first emanation of Prakriti in its undeveloped (Avyakta) state, be replaced by Sattvam Uttamam, the Highest Being? The word may be meant for Buddhi, for Buddhi is often called Mahat, the Great, but why it should be called Great is difficult to say. It is certainly not an equivalent of the Phenician Mot, as Professor Wilson conjectured many years ago \ Mahan Atm& looks like a Ved&ntie term, but even then it would only occupy the place of Givatma, the indi- 1 See Sawkhya-Sutras I, 61, 71 ; the Ekadasakam is Sattvikam, ef. II, 18, that is the five Buddhindriyas, the five Karmendriyas, and the Manas ; see Qarbe, Samkhya-prava&ana-bhashya, p, 188.
S a 260 INDIAN PHILOSOPHY.
vidualised Self, and how could this be said to emanate from the Avyakta?

Another passage which reminds us of S&mkhya rather than of Vedanta-philosophy occurs in the Maitray. Up. II, 5, where we read : ' He who has the name of Purusha, and is very small, intangible, invisible, dwells of his own will l here in part 2, as a man who is fast asleep awakes of his own will. And this part, which is entirely intelligent, present in every single man, knowing the body, attested by conceiving (Manas), willing (Buddhi), and belief in subject and object (Ahamkara) is Pra^&pati, called Visva. By him, the intelligent, is the body made intelligent, and he is the driver thereof/ This passage does not contain much of Samkhya thought, yet the words Purusha and possibly Buddhipurvam seem to allude to Kapila's ideas rather than to those of Badarliya?ia. Other words also, such as Sam/kalpa, Adhyavas&ya and Abhimana, in the sense of Ahamk&ra, point to the same source. The whole passage, however, is obscure, nor does the commentator help us much, unless he is right in recognising here the germs of the later Vedantic ideas of a Pra^apati, called Visva or Vaisv&nara (Ved&nta-sara, 138), Tai(/asa and Pr&gwa.

One more passage of the Maitray. Upanishad, III, 2, may here be mentioned, as reminding us of S&mkhya doctrines. There we read: 'There is indeed that other different one, called the elemental Self (Bhutatma) who, overcome by the bright and dark fruits of action, enters on a good or evil birth, so that his course is upward or downward, and that overpowered by the pairs (the opposites) he roams about. And this is the explanation. The five Tanmatras (of sound, touch, light, taste, and smell) are called Bhftta (elements), and the five MaMbhutas (gross elements) also are called Bhilta. Then the aggregate of all these is called /Skrlra, body, and he who dwells in that body is called Bhut&tman (the elementary Atman). True, his 1 The Amibhuti-prakasa reads Buddhipurvam ; I>etissen translates Abiaddiupurvam.
3 As to the idea of parts (Awisa)? see Vedanta-Sutras II, 3, 43, and Thlbaut's remarks in his Introduction, p. xcvii.

RELATION BETWEEN VEDANTA AND SAJfKHYA. 261 immortal Atman (Self) remains untainted, like a drop of water on a lotus-leaf ; but he, the Bhiitatman, is in the power of the Gu-nas of Prakriti. Then, thus overpowered, he becomes bewildered, and because thus bewildered, he sees not the creator, i.e. the holy Lord, abiding within him. Carried along by the Gunas, darkened, unstable, fickle, crippled, full of devices, vacillating, he enters into Abhimana (conceit of subject and object), believing "I am he, this is mine," &c. He binds himself by himself, as a bird is bound by a net, and, overcome afterwards by the fruits of what he has done, he enters on a good or evil birth, downward or upward in his course, and, overcome by the pairs, he roams about/ Here we see again a mixture of Samkhya and Vedanta ideas, the Samkhya claiming such terms as Prakriti and Guftas, the Vedanta such terms as Atman and possibly Bhutatman. This Bhutatman, however, is by no means so clear as has sometimes been imagined. It is a term peculiar to the Maitr&y. Upanishad, and seems 'to have been borrowed from it when it occurs in some of the later Upanishads. If, like many other things in the Maitray. Upanishad, it is to be looked upon as belonging to the Samkhya-system, we must remember that Atman, though quoted sometimes as a synonym of Purusha, cannot be supposed to stand here for Purusha. A compound such as Bhuta-Purusha would be impossible. The Maitray. Up. Ill, i itself says that the^ Atman of Bhutatman is another, though likewise called Atman, and that he dwells in the body, $arira, which is a compound of TanmMras, Bhutas, and Mah&bhutas. It would therefore correspond to the Ved&ntie Crivatman. But if this Bhutatman is said to spring from PrakHti, it could not possibly stand for the Purusha of the Samkhyas, because their Purusha does not spring from Prak?'iti, as little as Prakriti springs from him. Nor could any Atman be said to be purely objective. In fact, strictly speaking, this Bhutatman fits neither into the Vedanta, nor into the S&mkhya-philosophy, and would rather seem to belong to a philosophy in which these two views of the world were not yet finally separated.
Another difficult and rather obscure expression in the 262 INDIAN PHILOSOPHY.
Maitray. Upanishad is Niratman (selbstlos), an expression which would be impossible in the Vedanta-philosophy, and is certainly perplexing even in the Samkhya.

A similar mixture of philosophical terms meets us in the $vetasvatara Upanishad. In verse I, 10, for instance, we have Pradhana, which is Samkhya, and M&y&, which is Vedanta, at least the later Ved&nta, while in IV, 10 Maya is directly identified with Prakriti. Purusha occurs in III, 13, where it evidently stands for Brahman, IV, i. But though in this Upanishad S&mkhya ideas would seem to prevail, Vedanta ideas are not excluded. The very name of Sa/mkhya 1 and Yoga occurs (VI, 13), but the name of Vedanta also is not absent, VI, 22. In all this we may possibly get a glimpse of a state of Indian, philosophy which was, as yet, neither pure Samkhya nor pure Ved&nta, unless we look on these Upanishads as* of a far more modern date, and on their philosophy as the result of a later syncretism.

IV. If now we return to the Tattva-samasa, we meet first of all with some more remarks about the three Guwas, Sattva, explained as virtue, purity, goodness ; Ragras, explained as dust, mist, passion, movement, and Tamas, darkness, as ignorance. Colebrooke had already warned us against taking the Gunas of the S&mkhya in the sense of qualities. 'These three qualities/ he says, 'are not mere accidents of nature, but are of its essence, and enter into its composition like different rivers forming one stream, though for a time retaining their different colours/ Constituent * parts ' might be a better rendering, but for the present it is best to retain Gum, there being neither thought nor word in English corresponding to Gur^a, as defined in the Samkhya. We ourselves have inherited our ideas of substance and quality from Greek and medieval philosophers, but even with us a definition of inherent qualities is by no means easy, considering that our substances never exist 1 Samkhya should be here taken as the title of the two systems, Sawkhya and Yoga, or better still as one word, Sawkhyayoga. It cannot well mean Prufung.
TBAIGUOTA. 263 without qualities, nor our qualities without substances. Our commentary continues :—
He now asks, What is the triad of Gu-nas ? and the answer is, the triad consists of Goodness, Passion, and Darkness. The triad of Gu?ms means the three Gunas.
zh: 6 《梵點奧義書》12。6 參照《薄伽梵歌》V, T& S 258 《印度哲學》。
在蚯蚓和昆蟲中，在被棄絕者中，在狗和象中，在野獸、牛、牛虻和蚊蟲中，以及在珍珠、寶石、珊瑚、陶瓷和白銀中，這樣同一個自我（Self）即應被認識為遍居於牛、人和象、鹿等之中。
我們在這些節選中看到吠檀多（Vedānta）與數論（Sāṃkhya）術語與思想的混合；在第十頌中，梵（Brahman）為一與原人（Purusha）為多這兩種觀點被同時闡述。
吠檀多與數論之間的早期關係

在奧義書時期，數論派（Samkhya）與吠檀多派（Vedanta）之間的關係絕非清晰明瞭。多數學者似乎傾向於將其視為某種折衷主義，但它也可能代表著一個哲學思想時期，在這個時期，這兩種世界觀尚未最終分化，也未被感受為完全不相容。雖然我們所擁有的奧義書中明顯偏向於吠檀多派對世界的詮釋，但當數論派哲學家主張他們的觀點也可由吠陀權威來支持時，他們並非全然錯誤。這些觀點最初不過是對真理的猜測，是在黑暗中摸索；但若一個觀點正確，另一個必然錯誤的想法，明確地屬於較晚的時期，即系統化與論辯哲學的時代。有若干術語，如普魯沙（Purusha）、覺識（Buddhi）、三性質（Guṇas）等，被視為數論派的特有產業，而其他術語，如阿特曼（Atman）、梵（Brahman）、無明（Avidyā）、摩耶（Māyā）等，則立即使我們想起吠檀多派哲學；但即便是這些術語，在梵書與奧義書中的使用也遠比在哲學系統（Darsanas）中自由得多，而且早期與後期的權威對它們的使用意義和順序也並非始終一致。
因此我們在《咖打咖奧義書》（Kaṭhaka Up.）第三章第10節讀到：
感官之外是對象（Artha），對象之外是心（Manas），心之外是智慧（Buddhi），大我（Maha Atma）超越於智慧之外。大我之外是未顯現者（Avyakta），未顯現者之外是原人（Purusha）。原人之外別無他物，此乃目標，至高之點。
在同一部奧義書（Upanishad）第六卷第七、八章中，我們讀到：
最高的存在體（Sattvam Uttamam），高於此存在體的是偉大的自我（Mahan Atma），超越此偉大自我的是最高者，即未分化者。
超越未開展者之外是普魯沙（Purusha），遍佈一切且難以察知。每一個認識他的生物都獲得解脫，並獲得不朽。如此所述的逐次發展，雖然與系統化的數論派（Samkhya）並不完全一致，但更不能代表吠檀多的思想。即使同一奧義書中所給出的兩種說法，也略有差異，這向我們表明在奧義書時期技術準確性是何等匱乏。參見——III, 10, ii. VI, 7, 8。
感官。感官。
2. 意義（Arthas）。
3. 心識（Manas）。心識（Manas）。
4. 覺慧（Buddhi）。「薩特瓦尼‧烏塔瑪」（Sattvani Uttamam）。
5. 摩訶阿特曼（Mahan Atma）
6. 無顯現者（Avyakta）。無顯現者（Avyakta）。
七、普魯沙（Purusha）。普魯沙（Purusha）。

如果省略阿爾塔（Arthas）作為對象，這並無大礙，因為作為感官對象（Indriyarthas），它們已為感官或諸根（Indriyas）所暗示。然而，為何應將佛地（Buddhi）——通常是未顯現（Avyakta）狀態下實性（Prakriti）的最初流溢——替換為最高存有（Sattvam Uttamam）呢？該詞可能意指佛地，因為佛地常被稱為摩訶（Mahat），即「大者」，但為何應稱之為「大者」實難言之。它肯定不是腓尼基人的莫特（Mot）的對應物，如威爾遜教授多年前所推測的。摩訶阿特曼（Mahan Atm&）看起來像是韋檀多派的術語，但即便如此，它也只會佔據生命自我（Givatma）的位置，個別靈魂或個我的代稱。¹ ¹ 見《數論經》（Sawkhya-Sutras）I, 61, 71；十一組（Ekadasakam）是薩埃瓦派的（Sattvikam），參照 II, 18，亦即五感知根（五 Buddhindriyas）、五行動根（五 Karmendriyas）及意根（Manas）；見卡貝（Qarbe），《數論光明疏》（Samkhya-prava&ana-bhashya），第 188 頁。
第 260 頁 印度哲學
個別化的自我，以及這怎麼可能被說是從阿毘亞克塔（Avyakta）中流出呢？

邁特瑞耶奧義書（Maitray. Up.）第二卷第五章出現另一段文字，其精神更接近數論學派（Sāṃkhya）而非吠檀多哲學，文中記載：「那位名為普魯沙（Purusha）者，極其微小、無形、無色，居住於此，按其本願；如同一個熟睡的人按其本願而甦醒。而這個部分，完全具有智慧性，臨現於每個人之中，認識身體，由心意（Manas）之思量、理智（Buddhi）之志願，以及我執（Ahamkāra）之主客體信念所證成，乃是梵天主（Prajāpati），稱為毗瑞瓦（Visva）。由他，這有智慧者，身體得以有智慧，而他乃是其駕馭者。」此段文字雖然不含太多數論思想，然而普魯沙（Purusha）之詞，以及或許還有「以理智為先」（Buddhipurvam）之說，似乎暗指卡皮拉（Kapila）之觀點而非婆達利耶那（Bādarāyaṇa）之說。其他詞語，如三摩迦爾巴（Samkalpa）、阿地雅瓦薩雅（Adhyavasāya）及阿比曼那（Abhimāna），皆在我執（Ahamkāra）之義而言，亦指向同一源頭。然而整段文義晦澀，其註疏家亦幫助不大，除非他在此認出後世吠檀多關於梵天主（Prajāpati）─稱為毗瑞瓦（Visva）或毗瑞瓦納羅（Vaiśvānara）─泰依薩（Taijasa）及帕拉格瓦（Prāgjña）之思想萌芽乃為正確（吠檀多薩拉（Vedānta-sāra），§ 138）。

《彌多羅耶尼奧義書》（Maitray. Upanishad）第三卷第二章中，還有一段文字值得在此提及，它令人想起數論派（Sāṃkhya）的教義。其中記載：「確實有另一個不同的存在，被稱為元素自我（Bhutatma），他被業果中光明與黑暗的部分所克服，進入善業或惡業的轉生，因此他的路途或向上或向下，且被對立面（對偶性）所制服而四處漂泊。這是其解釋。五種微質（Tanmatras）（聲、觸、光、味、嗅）被稱為微質（Bhūta），五種大微質（Mahābhūta）（粗質元素）也被稱為微質。那麼所有這些的總體被稱為身體（Śarīra），而居住在該身體中的者被稱為元素自我（Bhūtātman）。確實，他的」¹ ¹《阿毗布蒂般若波羅蜜多經》（Amṛbhuti-prakāśa）讀作 Buddhipurvam；德泰森（Deitssen）譯作 Abiaddiupurvam。
關於部分的觀念（Awisa），見《韋檀多經》II, 3, 43，及蒂博的《導論》第 xcvii 頁的相關評論。

不朽的阿特曼（自我）保持純淨，如蓮葉上的水珠；但這位物質阿特曼（Bhutatman）卻受制於實質（Prakriti）的三德（Gunas）。於是，被三德所壓制，他變得迷惑，因而迷惑，他看不見住於他內心的造物者，即至聖的主。被三德所驅使，被黑暗籠罩，不穩定、多變、殘缺、詭計多端、猶豫不決，他進入了我執（Abhimana），相信「我就是他，這是我的」等等。他以自己束縛自己，如同一隻鳥被網所困；其後被自己所作所為的果報所制伏，他進入善業或惡業之生，在其過程中向下或向上，被對立二元所克服，他四處漂泊。 在此我們再次看到數論與吠檀多思想的混合，數論主張諸如實質與三德這樣的術語，吠檀多主張如阿特曼，以及可能的物質阿特曼這樣的術語。然而，這個物質阿特曼絕非如有時所想象的那樣清晰。它是《邁特雷耶奧義書》所獨有的術語，似乎是從該書中借用而來，當它出現在某些後出的奧義書中時。如果像《邁特雷耶奧義書》中許多其他事物一樣，它應當被看作屬於數論體系，我們必須記住，阿特曼雖然有時被引述為普魯沙（Purusha）的同義詞，但不能被設想為在此代表普魯沙。物質普魯沙（Bhuta-Purusha）這樣的複合詞是不可能的。《邁特雷耶奧義書》第三篇第一章本身說，物質阿特曼的阿特曼是另一個，儘管同樣被稱為阿特曼，他住於身體（Sharira）中，這是細微元素（Tanmatras）、粗大元素（Bhutas）和大元素（Mahabhutas）的複合體。因此它應當相應於吠檀多的肉身阿特曼（Sthvatman）。但如果這個物質阿特曼據說源出於實質，它就不可能代表數論派的普魯沙，因為他們的普魯沙並非源自實質，就如同實質也不源自他。也沒有任何阿特曼可以說是純粹客觀的。事實上，嚴格而言，這個物質阿特曼既不適應於吠檀多，也不適應於數論哲學，而寧可似乎屬於一個世界觀的哲學，在該哲學中，這兩種觀點還未最終分離。
另一個困難且相當晦澀的表述在262 印度哲學。
《彌勒奧義書》是無我論（Niratman）的，這樣的表達方式在吠檀多哲學中是不可能的，即使在數論派中也肯定令人困惑。

在《白馬奧義書》（Śvetāśvatara Upanishad）中，我們同樣遇見哲學術語的混合。例如在第一篇第十節，我們有「原質」（Pradhana）——屬於數論派（Samkhya）——以及「幻力」（Māyā）——屬於吠檀多派（Vedanta），至少是後期的吠檀多派，而在第四篇第十節，幻力（Maya）被直接等同於「實質」（Prakriti）。「人我」（Purusha）出現在第三篇第十三節，其中顯然代表梵我（Brahman），見第四篇第一節。但儘管在此奧義書中數論派（Sāmkhya）思想似乎佔據優勢，吠檀多派思想並未被排除。數論派與瑜伽派的名稱本身都出現了（第六篇第十三節），但吠檀多派的名稱也並未缺席，見第六篇第二十二節。在所有這些之中，我們或許可以窺見印度哲學的某一階段——它既非純粹的數論派，亦非純粹的吠檀多派——除非我們將這些奧義書視為時代遠為晚近，其哲學為後來的融合主義（syncretism）之結果。

若我們現今回歸《本質論》（Tattva-samasa），首先會遇到關於三德（Gunas）的一些進一步論述——薩埵（Sattva），解釋為德行、純淨、善良；羅闍（Rajas），解釋為塵埃、迷霧、欲望、運動；以及闇摩（Tamas），黑暗，解釋為無明。科爾布魯克（Colebrooke）已經警告我們，不要以質的意義來理解數論派（Samkhya）的德（Gunas）。他說：「這三德並非自然的純粹附屬物，而是自然的本質所在，進入其組成如同不同的河流匯成一道水流，雖然暫時保留各自的顏色。」「本質成分」可能是更恰當的譯法，但目前最好還是保留德（Guna）一詞，既然英文中既無思想也無詞彙與數論派所定義的德（Guña）相對應。我們自身承襲了關於實質與質性的觀念來自希臘及中世紀哲學家，但即使以我們而言，內在質性的定義也絕非易事，考慮到我們的實質從未*單獨存在。¹ --- ¹ 此處應理解「數論派」（Samkhya）為兩大系統——數論派與瑜伽派——的總稱，或更準確地說為一個詞「數論瑜伽」（Samkhyayoga）。它不宜解作「檢驗」（Prüfung）。
無質的物質，也無離物質的質性。我們的評註如下所述：—
他現在提問：古那三性（triad of Gu-nas）是什麼？答案是，這個三性包括善性、激性與暗性。古那三性（the triad of Gu-nas）意指三種古那（three Gunas）。
